headline: NVDA Q1 FY27 smashes consensus: $81.6B rev (+85% YoY), datacenter $75.2B (+92%), Q2 guide $91B vs Street $86.8B; $80B new buyback, dividend hiked 25x to $0.25
summary: Nvidia reported fiscal Q1 2027 Wednesday after the bell: revenue $81.62B beat the $78.89B consensus, non-GAAP EPS $1.87 vs $1.76 Street, GAAP EPS $2.39. Datacenter revenue hit a record $75.2B, up 92% YoY, with hyperscalers >50% of segment at $38B (+12% QoQ). Non-GAAP gross margin 75.0% — at the high end of guide. Q2 FY27 revenue guide $91.0B ±2% blew past the $86.84B LSEG consensus (and above the $90B whisper). Capital return: board authorized an incremental $80B buyback (no expiration) and hiked the quarterly cash dividend 25x from $0.01 to $0.25. CEO Jensen Huang called the AI-factory buildout 'the largest infrastructure expansion in human history,' and the company unveiled the Vera Rubin platform — including the Vera CPU 'purpose-built for agentic AI' — plus BlueField-4 STX storage IP and the GA of Dynamo 1.0 (7x inference speedup on Blackwell). Stock paradoxically fell ~1% in after-hours despite the comprehensive beat as the bar had ratcheted; setup heading into Thursday is buy-side de-risk vs. the analyst PT raise wave.

headline: Sell-side floods the tape with NVDA price-target raises: Baird $500 (from $300), Evercore $413, Benchmark $335, BofA $350, Wedbush $330, MS $288, RBC $270, GS $285
summary: Within hours of the print every major desk lifted NVDA targets. Most aggressive: Baird to $500 (from $300, +67%), Evercore ISI to $413 (from $352), Benchmark to $335 (from $250). Bulge-bracket: BofA's Vivek Arya to $350 (from $320), Morgan Stanley's Joe Moore to $288 (from $285, reiterated Top Pick), Goldman's Toshiya Hari to $285 (from $250), Wedbush's Dan Ives to $330 (from $300), RBC to $270 (from $250). HSBC's prior $325 (set May 19) and Jefferies' $275 remain anchor points. Consensus mean target now ~$291 (Strong Buy from 61 polled). Net read: every model gets re-rated higher off the $91B Q2 guide; the only debate is whether the Rubin-cycle terminal multiple holds vs. macro discount-rate compression. NVDA pre-market trading roughly flat after AH fade as the macro tape (Iran oil, 30Y yields) overrides idiosyncratic.

headline: Samsung 18-day strike begins Day 1 (May 21): ~45,000+ unionized workers off; HBM lines on emergency staffing; JPM models $14-20.8B operating-profit hit
summary: The largest labor walkout in Samsung Electronics' history begins Thursday May 21 after final mediation collapsed last week. ~45,000-61,000 of ~50,000 unionized workers are participating (93.1% strike authorization) and the action is scheduled to run 18 days through June 7. The Monday court injunction protecting 7,087 safety/wafer-line staff means HBM4 throughput at Pyeongtaek is partially insulated, but R&D, equipment moves, and node migrations are exposed. Cost models: JPMorgan $14-20.8B operating profit hit (full duration), industry estimates ~$700M/day in sales, worst-case scenarios up to KRW 30T (~$22B). TrendForce: 3-4% of global DRAM and ~3% of NAND capacity at risk; Jefferies sees order migration to MU and 000660.KS. Samsung has been running 'warm-down' wafer cuts since May 14 and shifting product mix toward HBM to triage the impact. Sympathy bid: 000660.KS, MU, STX expected to open firm; KOSPI futures pre-open weak. SK Hynix per-capita bonuses (~700M won) are >2x Samsung's — root cause of the dispute and the engineer attrition that preceded it.

headline: FOMC April 28-29 minutes show 8-4 vote — most divided since October 1992; Miran dissented for a 25bp cut while three hawks dissented on cut-bias language
summary: The April FOMC minutes (released 2pm ET Wed May 20) confirmed an unusually fragmented committee. The decision to hold the federal funds rate at 3.50-3.75% drew four dissents — the largest split in over 30 years. Governor Miran voted to cut 25bps citing growth-side risk; three other officials objected to language hinting at eventual cut bias. Per the minutes, equity prices had 'more than reversed earlier declines' over the intermeeting period while 2Y/10Y yields rose along with near-term inflation compensation; corporate spreads narrowed. Read-through: the path-of-least-resistance for the next meeting (June) is hold, but the tail of 'hike if Iran-driven inflation persists' lives explicitly inside the minutes. Fed funds futures now price 48% odds of a hike by December (was 14% a week ago); BofA still calls no cuts until H2 2027. Long-end yields pulled back modestly post-release (30Y from 5.198% peak, 10Y to 4.67%), but the structural 5%+ pricing on the 30Y remains intact — keeps the discount-rate cap on long-duration AI names (CRWV, OKLO, SMR).

headline: Crude reverses higher pre-open: Iran Supreme Leader orders enriched uranium kept in-country; Trump issues 2-3 day ultimatum; Brent +1% to $112, WTI back above $103
summary: Oil flipped from Wednesday's 2% retreat after Reuters reported Iran's Supreme Leader directed all enriched uranium remain inside Iran — a material setback to the 14-point MOU framework that had reopened Hormuz transit talk. Trump told reporters Iran has 'two or three days, maybe Friday, Saturday, Sunday, something' to agree to a deal, raising the probability of a renewed US strike. Three supertankers cleared the Strait Wednesday but the route remains largely restricted. Pre-open prints: Brent +1% near $112, WTI back above $103, both reversing Wednesday's de-escalation rally. S&P futures -0.45%, Nasdaq -0.50%, Dow -0.48% as macro tone turns risk-off. Direct read-across on dashboard: NVDA cannot fully translate the print into upside in this tape; CEG/VST/OKLO get a relative baseload bid; CRWV/BE see rate-duration pressure return. Russell 2000 is the one bright spot at +2.56% as small-caps catch the FOMC-dissent dovish-tail bid.

headline: GE Vernova Q1 datacenter equipment orders ($2.4B) eclipsed all of 2025; gas-turbine lead times now stretch to 7 years as Siemens Energy/Mitsubishi can't keep pace
summary: GE Vernova (GEV) booked $2.4B in electrification-segment orders for datacenters in Q1 2026 alone — more than all of 2025 combined — and signed 21 GW of new gas-turbine agreements in the quarter. Industry-wide, turbine wait times have stretched to up to seven years per power-eng reporting; the GEV/Siemens Energy/MHI triad accounts for >70% of global production capacity and is still falling behind hyperscaler demand. Pricing power is now structural: every new 1 GW datacenter campus competing against utilities for the same generator slot. This dovetails with the $725B 2026 hyperscaler capex stack reported last week. Direct beneficiaries on dashboard: GEV (gas + grid), ETN (electrification, 240% YoY datacenter orders, $22.8B backlog), VRT (rack-level power, FY26 guide $13.5-14.0B, +115% YTD), Schneider (SBGSY), ABB (NVDA 800V DC partnership). Power-density and lead-time scarcity is the binding constraint into 2027-28.

headline: CoreWeave taps $3.1B GPU-backed delayed-draw term loan to fund AI cloud expansion; total contracted power now 3.5 GW, backlog $99.4B
summary: CoreWeave (CRWV) finance subsidiary closed a $3.1B delayed-draw term loan facility collateralized by GPU infrastructure, with draws available through September 2026 and final maturity November 2031. Proceeds fund GPU buildout tied to two large non-investment-grade customer contracts plus general AI-cloud expansion. The company reported 3.5 GW total contracted power and a $99.4B revenue backlog (largest contracted-revenue position among public AI infrastructure names) at Q1; >75% of 2027 capacity is already pre-sold and management is targeting >$30B FY27 annualized run-rate. Layering with the broader $725B hyperscaler capex stack, CRWV sits at the leveraged tip of the AI infrastructure spear — fully sold-out FY26, negative net income, heavy capex, and rate-sensitive equity. Yield backup since FOMC minutes is the headline risk; positive NVDA print is the counterweight. Watch for sell-side commentary tying the CRWV thesis to NVDA Q2 $91B guide.

headline: Tokyo Electron Taiwan drops appeal in TSMC trade-secrets case after court fines unit and jails ex-engineer; clean read for ASML/AMAT/LRCX as TSMC tightens supplier discipline
summary: Tokyo Electron's Taiwan subsidiary (8035.T) said May 21 it would respect judicial proceedings and not appeal a ruling in the TSMC trade-secrets case, after a Taiwanese court fined the local unit and sentenced an ex-engineer to prison. The outcome closes a multi-year overhang and signals TSMC is willing to use the legal system to enforce supplier-side IP discipline — net positive for WFE peers (ASML, AMAT, LRCX, KLAC) who benefit from a level playing field, and reduces TEL execution risk on the $52-56B 2026 TSMC capex pull-through. Morgan Stanley earlier this week lifted ASML to EUR 1,400 (from EUR 1,000) and UBS named ASML top European semi pick at EUR 1,900 — the underlying capex setup remains the bull case. Tokyo Electron last traded JPY ~32,000 pre-news.
